summary: 1948 Italian neorealist film by Vittorio De Sica. Antonio Ricci pawns his wife Maria's dowry bedsheets to reclaim his bicycle, needed for a job posting film posters; the bicycle is stolen the very next day. He and his son Bruno search Rome. Film ends with Antonio caught trying to steal an unattended bicycle, humiliated before Bruno. Known for the restaurant scene cutting between poor Antonio/Bruno and a wealthy family. Screenwriter: Cesare Zavattini. Often called one of the greatest films ever made.

works.name: Plot and Characters
works.description: Antonio Ricci (played by Lamberto Maggiorani) needs a bicycle to work posting film posters (including of Rita Hayworth). His wife Maria pawns her dowry bedsheets to get the bicycle out of a pawn shop. The next day, the bicycle is stolen while Antonio is putting up posters. Antonio and his son Bruno search Rome. Antonio tails an old man to a church during a rainstorm. A suspect fakes a seizure to avoid arrest. A fortune teller says Antonio will find the bicycle quickly or not at all. Antonio ends up trying to steal an unattended bicycle himself and is mobbed by police. In the final scene, Antonio and Bruno walk off crying into the crowd. Wikipedia

works.name: Production and Context
works.description: Directed by Vittorio De Sica, written by Cesare Zavattini. A masterpiece of Italian neorealism. The bicycle is a 'Fides' brand. An anti-commercialist parody by comedian Maurizio Nichetti is based on this film (a 1989 parody in which frequent commercial breaks and a power outage disrupt a director's reinterpretation). Influenced Indian Parallel Cinema, including Satyajit Ray's Apu Trilogy and Bimal Roy's Do Bigha Zamin.

clues.clue: Directed by Vittorio De Sica; screenplay by Cesare Zavattini; landmark of Italian neorealism
examples: name this Vittorio De Sica film in which Bruno helps his father Antonio recover a stolen vehicle, a masterpiece of neorealism
Bicycle Thieves is perhaps the best-known work by this Italian director, whose other films include Sciuscia and Umberto D. He often collaborated with Cesare Zavattini

comprehensive_summary:
Bicycle Thieves (Ladri di biciclette, 1948) is a landmark Italian neorealist film directed by Vittorio De Sica with a screenplay by Cesare Zavattini. The film follows Antonio Ricci, a poor Roman laborer who is offered work posting film posters — including of Rita Hayworth — on the condition that he has a bicycle. His wife Maria pawns her dowry bedsheets to redeem Antonio's bicycle from a pawn shop. The very next day, while Antonio is up a ladder posting bills, his bicycle (a 'Fides' brand) is stolen. Antonio and his young son Bruno spend the rest of the film searching Rome.

During a rainstorm, Antonio tails a suspicious old man to a church serving lunch. A suspect later fakes a seizure to avoid arrest. A fortune teller tells Antonio he will find the bicycle either quickly or not at all. A restaurant scene memorably intercuts between the poor father-son duo discussing their plight and a wealthy family eating lavishly. At the end of the film, Antonio is reduced to attempting to steal an unattended bicycle himself — in an inversion of the opening dynamic — and is caught and humiliated before Bruno. The two walk off into the crowd crying.

The film is widely called one of the greatest ever made and a masterpiece of Italian neorealism. De Sica's other key neorealist films include Sciuscia and Umberto D. A 1989 anti-commercialist parody by comedian Maurizio Nichetti (in which commercial breaks and a power outage disrupt a director's reinterpretation) is based on this film. Bicycle Thieves also influenced the Indian Parallel Cinema movement, including Satyajit Ray's Apu Trilogy and Bimal Roy's Do Bigha Zamin.
